properties.artistprojectstatement:
Garnished sundries presented on twenty transit-shelter posters throughout the city from January 3 - 30, 2022. 

Through this new photographic series of intimate household objects that have been cut up, collaged and reorganized, Svava Tergesen hopes to create an experience where the public can consider how domestic life has changed and shape-shifted throughout this pandemic.  

Tergesen was inspired by the history of the still-life — in which sumptuous arrangements of foods, glassware, pottery and plants were carefully rendered as paintings. Riffing on these conventions, Tergesen’s work displays mutated objects that are no longer whole, but have been transformed into new hybrid creations. The photographs ask questions of the viewer: what differentiates a chore such as meal preparation from an act of play? How can the home be used or seen in new ways?

properties.artistprojectstatement:
Waiting Room, Alex MacKenzie’s material-based film work, speaks to the various states of tension, calm, frustration, apprehension, injustice, inequity, and struggle felt in our respective waiting rooms. 16mm found footage is manipulated using optical printing, layering and reprocessing.

The simultaneous banality and stress of waiting undergoes a physical transformation into an active and highly charged materiality informed by our personal projections and desires.

properties.artistprojectstatement:
Domestic Cosmology presented on twenty transit-shelter posters throughout the city from September 5 - October 2, 2022. 

During the pandemic when the artist Jem Noble had to remove the surface of his home/studio floor following water damage, he was awed to find a sea of stones of many different shapes, sizes and hues, embedded in concrete—a found portrait of the Milky Way. In this time when many people’s lives were condensed into domestic spheres, the galaxy beneath his feet reminded him that isolation is a complex feature of cosmic interdependence.  Domestic Cosmology, a poster depicting a distorted and functional QR code, will connect audiences to a video and music composition that the artist created from his own revelation of perspective on the cosmic continuum.

properties.artistprojectstatement:
Vacant Lots I-IV presented on twenty transit-shelter posters throughout the city from July 4 - 31, 2022.

This series of unique prints combines the traditional printmaking processes of monotype, silkscreen, and stone lithography. Working loosely from satellite images of empty parking lots within the city of Vancouver and improvisational marks made during the printing process, these Vacant Lots portray both a timeline of activity and a pause. The prints recall feelings of isolation, confinement, and emptiness, similar to when the world seemed to pause in March of 2020. On the surface they are uninhabited but when viewed from above, they embody an organized togetherness and signal a presence within a field of absence.

properties.artistprojectstatement:
Untitled was exhibited on the Marine Drive Canada Line Station from June 2023 - June 2024.

Lauren Crazybull writes about her work: “What relationship do Indigenous people have to the eternal? At some point, it was predicted that Indigenous people were disappearing and would live on only in anthropological representations—ones that quantified and measured us and left us forever in a state of romanticization. Indigenous complexity was often erased in these portrayals. Temporally we are expected to represent a time that precedes us. My painted portraits have been a way to establish Indigenous presence in a way that places us firmly in the present, to look forward to our futures. The people embodied within this artwork are Indigenous, belonging to these lands and spanning across to my home, the prairies. Thank you Elijah, Taryn, Xwalacktun, Mom, and Jordan for being a part of this work.”

label: Raven Searches for the Light, Raven Steals the Light, Raven and the Dying of the Light, and Raven Becoming the Light

properties.title_of_work: Raven Searches for the Light, Raven Steals the Light, Raven and the Dying of the Light, and Raven Becoming the Light
properties.artistprojectstatement:
This artwork presented on transit shelters throughout the city from September 25th to November 5th, 2023.

K.C. Hall has created a series of graphic comic book covers that centre around his interpretation of four Heiltsuk raven legends.

The individual pieces, Raven Searches for Light, Raven Steals the Light, Raven the Dying Light, and Raven Becoming the Light, document Raven’s search for community, life, and meaning.

The raven character is also a self-portrait series detailing K.C.’s journey in finding out who he was as an artist. If you look closely at the posters around town you will see the different influences of stylized drawing, graffiti, urban noir, and Northwest Coast formline.

Raven Steals the Light reveals the artist’s sense of humour – with the double entendre of the smoking raven stealing someone’s lighter.

properties.artistprojectstatement:
This artwork was exhibited at the City Centre Canada Line Station from October 3, 2023 - March 15, 2024.

Raven N' The City is inspired by the long historical connection so many Haidas have with Vancouver and the xʷməθkʷəy̓əm (Musqueam), Sḵwx̱wú7mesh (Squamish), and səlil̓wətaʔɬ (Tsleil-Waututh) peoples, from pre-contact to our contemporary lives today.

Vancouver is a cultural hub for many Indigenous communities. Growing up, traveling to Vancouver was known as the “Haida Holiday” or, “going to the big city!” This piece is inspired by the feeling of the rush of leaving our small communities to visit the city. For a lot of us, downtown is one of the first stops upon arrival. There's a moment where you stop and realize you're not at home and there is so much possibility of what to do outside of small-town life's routine. And no matter how many times we come back, it can always feel a little overwhelming with where to start first.

The design features Nang Kilslaas, also known as Raven, arriving in downtown Vancouver. Raven is in the process of transforming out of bird form into their human form, their true face exposed by the Raven mask opening, with human arms and hands taking shape from their wings. The shapes around Raven are abstract design forms to represent the green glass buildings of the city of Vancouver.

properties.artistprojectstatement:
Ovoid Destroyer was exhibited on the Vanlive! video screen at the corner of Granville and Robson. Various times throughout the day with a dedicated showing at 12 noon, daily from Month/Year until March 16, 2025.

From the droplets by which these motions themselves are formed, this “Space Invader” inspired animation seeks the spatial territories of which it witnesses and protects. In understanding the amalgamation of internationally renowned aesthetics that compile “The Pacific Northwest Coast” do we realize the distances between invitation and occupation. Since time immemorial crescents, trigonals, and circles have been part of the visual sovereignty of our greater Xwelmewx territories, so too does the Ovoid Destroyer arrive to ensure the same.

label: a:sum tu kwulasun, tun’ni u’ tatulut kwsu sulsalewh ct (look at the stars, learn from our ancestors)

properties.title_of_work: a:sum tu kwulasun, tun’ni u’ tatulut kwsu sulsalewh ct (look at the stars, learn from our ancestors)
properties.artistprojectstatement:
a:sum tu kwulasun, tun’ni u’ tatulut kwsu sulsalewh ct (look at the stars, learn from our ancestors) was exhibited on the Marine Drive Canada Line Station from June 2024 - June 2025.

“It is a privilege to live and to share my work on the territories of my relatives, the xʷməθkʷəy̓əm, Sḵwx̱wú7mesh, and səlilwətaɬ peoples, I want to hold my hands up to them and acknowledge them first.

This installation is a collage of Coast Salish narratives about joy, connection to place, cosmology, and the love of our ancestors. I have spent a lot of time recently looking at historical works of Coast Salish art that our ancestors made and in doing so I have been reminded of the strength of who we are and the strength of our stories. When I look at Coast Salish art that is anywhere from hundreds to thousands of years old, I see a continuous flow of thought and visual language that connects through to the work done by relations and fellow artists now.

Through this piece, I am celebrating the commonalities of culture, story, and lived experience between us as Coast Salish peoples and asking myself what it means to carry Coast Salish art as a xwulmuxw today.”
